data.title: Torex Gold plunges after shutting down Mexican mine following fatal incident
data.description:
Torex Gold Resources (OTCPK:TORXF) -12.4% in Friday's trading after saying it suspended operations at its El Limón Guajes underground mine in Mexico following the death of three workers due to carbon monoxide exposure.

A fourth contract employee was hospitalized but is expected to recover, the company said.

Torex (OTCPK:TORXF) said it has launched an investigation to determine the cause of the accident, which occurred during the December 5 day shift at the mine.

In addition to El Limón Guajes, Torex (OTCPK:TORXF) is poised to have another underground mine within Mexico's Guerrero gold belt, with the nearby Media Luna project nearing completion.

The new mine will be tied into the ELG complex, and combined are expected to deliver 450K-470K oz of gold production this year.

data.title: Bank of Marin Bancorp CFO to retire
data.description:
• Bank of Marin Bancorp (NASDAQ:BMRC) announced on Friday the retirement of its Executive Vice President, CFO and Principal Accounting Officer Tani Girton, effective Jan. 31, 2025.
  • Dave Bonaccorso, currently the bank's treasurer, has been named the successor. Bonaccorso will become the CFO and principal accounting officer, effective Jan. 2, 2025.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: S&P, Nasdaq rise, eye weekly gains as traders boost rate-cut bets after payrolls print
data.description:
Key U.S. equity averages moved mostly higher Friday, as odds of a December rate cut climbed following November's U.S. jobs report that points to a slowing yet solid labor market.

Gains for the S&P 500 (SP500) and the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) put the averages on course for new closing highs. This year's advances for large-cap stocks have been aided by the Federal Reserve starting its rate-cutting cycle that investors expect policymakers to continue this month.

The tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) +0.7%, and the S&P 500 (SP500) +0.3%. The Dow (DJI) turned lower, slipping -0.1%. The Nasdaq was in line for a weekly rise of ~3%, and while the Dow was looking at a small loss. A third-straight weekly gain was on tap for the S&P 500 (SP500).

November nonfarm payrolls came in at +227K versus the +211K consensus estimate. The unemployment rate rose to 4.2% from 4.1%, in-line with forecasts. October's +12K print was hit by hurricanes and a workers' strike at Boeing (BA).

"Looking at the three market average of ~ 160k jobs, we see a healthy, although weakening, job market," Chris Zaccarelli, chief investment officer at Northlight Asset Management, said Friday.

"Despite the strong headline number this morning, the Fed is likely to note the overall slowing in the job market and cut rates by 25 bps in two weeks, unless the next CPI report is white hot," he said.

The November Consumer Price Index inflation report is due next week. Northlight's base case is that the CPI won’t deliver a material surprise, Zaccarelli said. Odds jumped to ~87% for a quarter-point rate cut at the Fed's December 17-18 meeting, from Thursday's 71% probability, according to the CME FedWatch tool.

The latest payrolls report appears to show the labor market is in a "sustainable full-employment space," Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee said Friday at the bank's Annual Economic Outlook Symposium.

In the fixed-income market, the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) slipped 1 basis point to 4.16%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) was down 4 basis points at 4.09%.

In other economic news, consumer sentiment drifted up in December, even with higher year-ahead inflation expectations. The University of Michigan's U.S. Consumer Sentiment report came in at 74.0 versus the 73.0 consensus and 71.8 November levels.

Among active stocks, DocuSign (DOCU) +24.5% and Ulta Beauty (ULTA) +9.6% on strong quarterly results.

In the crypto market, bitcoin (BTC-USD) continued to trade below $100,000, as it hovers above $99,350.

data.title: Oshkosh could lose USPS contract to electrify fleet - reports
data.description:
Shares of Oshkosh Corporation (NYSE:OSK) were broad-sided on Friday as reports circulated that the incoming Trump administration would cancel the U.S. Postal Service’s contract with electric vehicle manufacturers including Oshkosh (OSK) and Ford Motor (F).

According to Reuters, the measure is part of a broader initiative by the incoming administration to reverse government subsidies enacted by President Biden to encourage the adoption of electric vehicles.

Sources cited by Reuters say Trump’s transition team is reviewing options to unwind the postal service’s multi-billion-dollar contracts with companies like Oshkosh (OSK) and Ford (F) to provide tens of thousands of BEVs and charging stations.

The postal service was awarded a $3B grant from the U.S. government last year to electrify its fleet with 66,000 EVs by 2028, of which 45,000 would come from Oshkosh (OSK), and the remainder from other automakers. The charging stations would be provided by ChargePoint (CHPT), Blink Charging (BLNK) and Siemens (OTCPK:SIEGY).

Severing these contracts would be “legally challenging,” Reuters says, as the USPS operates as an autonomous federal agency with its own governing board. Therefore, any measures to rescind government contracts would likely require an Executive Order from the incoming president.

Shares of Oshkosh (OSK) are down 5.7%, with Ford (F) reversing earlier gains to trade 0.4% higher. Shares of Blink (BLNK), ChargePoint (CHPT) and Siemens (OTCPK:SIEGY) are not reacting to the latest development.

data.title: Cleveland Fed's Hammack favors slower pace of rate cuts than she did in September
data.description:
Cleveland Fed President Beth Hammack favors taking a slow pace in adjusting the Federal Reserve's policy rate, she said Friday at the City Club of Cleveland, according to prepared text. The strength of the economy leads her to support a slower pace of rate cuts than she had expected in September when Fed officials last submitted their economic projections.

"To balance the need to maintain a modestly restrictive stance for monetary policy with the possibility that policy may not be far from neutral, I believe we are at or near the point where it makes sense to slow the pace of rate reductions," she said. Note that the neutral rate is the point at which the Fed's policy rate neither fuels nor hinders the U.S. economy.

"Moving slowly will allow us to calibrate policy to the appropriately restrictive level over time given the underlying strength in the economy," Hammack said. Her approach focuses on getting inflation down to the central bank's 2% goal.

"Resilient growth, a healthy labor market, and still-elevated inflation suggest to me that it remains appropriate to maintain a modestly restrictive stance for monetary policy for some time," Hammack said. "Such a policy stance will help to sustainably return inflation all the way back to 2% in a timely fashion."

Financial markets seem to be pricing in one rate cut between now and the end of January, and only a few more cuts by the end of 2025.

"This path is consistent with my current expectation for the funds rate, based on my forecast that features solid economic growth, a low unemployment rate, and gradual improvements in inflation," she said.

Still, the data can change between now and then, which could alter the policy path. "As long as inflation is above our objective and the labor market remains strong, my focus remains on finishing the task at hand. Admittedly, it’s a careful balancing act."

Breaking news... check back for updates.

data.title: Canadian Western Bank drops after delaying Q4 results
data.description:
• Canadian Western Bank (TSX:CWB:CA) sunk 10% after the bank delayed releasing its Q4 results.
  • The bank now expects to issue the results by the middle of the month, the bank said in a statement on Friday.
  • The delay comes after National Bank of Canada (OTCPK:NTIOF) agreed in June to acquire Canadian Western Bank (OTCPK:CBWBF) for C$5.0B ($3.6B) in a bid to bolster its domestic growth and expand services for Canadian banking and wealth management clients.
